The Nine Doors of the Body, the Four Doors of Speech, and the Two Doors of Mind

# The Nine Doors of the Body, the Four Doors of Speech, and the Two Doors of Mind

[](https://substack.com/@tseringdorji)

![Tsering Dorji's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VB0T!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faedf658f-bb6c-4511-9227-fcdbd4e7c7bd_768x773.jpeg)

### **Notes and Context**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The three doors of the vāyus—body, speech, and mind—are an important concept in Vajrayāna Buddhism. The following translation is based on Tāranātha’s commentary on the *Vasantatilakā* of Kṛṣṇācārya. According to this Cakrasaṃvara tradition, the yogi blocks the nine doors of the body and the four doors of speech, while opening the two doors of the mind.

The four doors of speech are identified with the four nāḍī-petals of the heart cakra, through which flow the natural elemental vāyus. The secondary vāyus, known as the vāyus of manifestation, flow through the nine bodily openings, the seven openings of the head and the two lower openings. Through these arise the various conceptual thoughts and sensory perceptions.

With regard to the two doors of the mind, two explanations are commonly encountered. In the first, the two doors are the forehead and the crown, corresponding to the two upper terminations of the central channel. In the second, the upper door is the crown and the lower door is the navel. Tāranātha, however, presents a different interpretation in which the two doors of the mind are located at the heart and navel cakras. The channel-knots (*granthi*) of the heart and navel nāḍī-cakras function as the door panels. When these knots are loosened or opened through yogic practice, the doors of the mind are said to open, allowing the vāyus to enter the central channel.

The passage translated below explains these three doors of the vāyus and their significance within the dzogrim practice of the Cakrasaṃvara tradition.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Jnvv!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe98bac1d-d9a2-496d-8d3b-6cfd6d749bca_473x535.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Jnvv!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe98bac1d-d9a2-496d-8d3b-6cfd6d749bca_473x535.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### Cakrasaṃvara Maṇḍala and the Body Maṇḍala

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The symbolism of the Cakrasaṃvara maṇḍala may also be understood in relation to the subtle body. The square palace with its four gates represents the body itself. The four yoginīs residing upon the four nāḍī-petals of the heart cakra correspond to the four channels of the heart through which flow the vāyus of the four elements. The four gates likewise symbolize these four channels.

The eight charnel grounds surrounding the palace represent the eight bodily openings, with the two nostrils counted as a single opening. The four urns filled with amṛta situated upon the four nāḍī-petals symbolize the bodhicitta bindu residing within the heart centre.

At the navel cakra burns the inner fire (*caṇḍālī* or *tummo*), personified as Vajravārāhī, who in this tradition is called Tilakā. Through yogic practice, this inner fire causes the white bodhicitta bindu at the heart, representing Cakrasaṃvara to melt and descend. This union of Tilakā and Cakrasaṃvara forms the symbolic meaning of the title *Vasantatilakā* (“Spring Tilakā”), where the warming power of the inner fire melts the nectar-like bodhicitta, just as the warmth of spring melts snow.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# TARANATHA’S TEXT

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## The Three Doors: Body, Speech, and Mind

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

There are the nine doors of the body, the four doors of speech, and the two doors of mind.

### (i) The Nine Doors of the Body

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

As the *Saṃpuṭa Tantra* states, “\[the house with\] a single pillar and nine doors.” In relation to the channels, the “single pillar” refers to the central channel; in relation to the body, it refers to the spinal column. The “nine doors” are the nine bodily openings. These openings are the principal gateways through which the vāyus that sustain and constitute the body move inward and outward. For this reason, they are known as the nine doors of the body.

### (ii) The Four Doors of Speech

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

There are four gates corresponding to the four nāḍī-petals in the four directions of the heart nāḍī-cakra. Through these four gates flow the vāyus of the four elements: from the east, the air of water; from the north, the air of air; from the west, the air of fire; and from the south, the air of earth. Since the vāyus that produce speech and vocal expression move inward and outward through these channels, they are known as the four doors of speech.

### (iii) The Two Doors of Mind

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The two channel-knots of the central channel at the heart and the navel nāḍī-cakra are like door-panels through which the prāṇa-vāyu and apāna-vāyu, the root of all the vāyus, move inward and outward. The hubs, or centres, of the heart and navel nāḍī-cakras are themselves these two doors.

Since the prāṇa-vāyu is connected with the vasanta \[spring-like bodhicitta or white bindu\] of the heart, it is called the “vāyu of the moon.” Since the apāna-vāyu is connected with the bindu at the navel and the caṇḍālī (Tummo, inner heat), it is called the “vāyu of the sun.”

Because prāṇa and apāna are the two principal vāyus at the time when consciousness departs and re-enters the body, and because the heart and navel are the gateways through which this movement occurs, these two are also called the doors of the mind.
